text:
Peri-operative fasting instructions are provided to reduce the risk of pulmonary aspiration during induction of anaesthesia. When a patient receives sedation or general anaesthesia, protective airway reflexes are diminished; if gastric contents are present, they may regurgitate and enter the lungs, potentially causing aspiration pneumonitis, hypoxaemia, or other serious complications. For this reason, patients must follow fasting guidance precisely.

A typical instruction set is as follows: no solid food, milk, infant formula, or nutritional supplements for at least six hours before the scheduled procedure. A light meal, such as toast, should still comply with this six-hour interval, while a fatty meal may require a longer fasting period. Clear fluids may usually be taken until two hours before arrival or anaesthesia; examples include water, black tea or coffee without milk, and pulp-free clear juice. Chewing gum, sweets, and smoking should also be avoided because they may stimulate gastric secretions.

Patients should continue essential medications only if specifically advised, usually with a small sip of water. They should also be told that fasting does not mean withholding all regular treatment without instruction. If the operation is delayed, staff will reassess whether additional clear fluids are appropriate. Clear, written instructions, confirmation of understanding, and attention to comorbidities such as diabetes, gastroparesis, pregnancy, or obesity are essential to safe peri-operative care.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text:
Before your operation, we need your stomach pretty much empty. The reason is simple: once you’re asleep for surgery, your body can’t protect your airway the way it normally does. If there’s food or drink sitting in your stomach, it can come back up and go into your lungs, which is exactly the kind of drama nobody wants on surgery day.

So here’s the usual rule of thumb: stop eating solid food about six hours before your procedure. That means no breakfast, no snacks, no toast, and no sneaky “just a bite.” Milk drinks and smoothies count as food here, so they’re out too. Clear drinks are usually okay until two hours before surgery. Think water, black coffee, black tea, or clear apple juice without bits. A latte is not a clear drink, and neither is orange juice with pulp.

If you take regular medicines, don’t just guess. Some pills should still be taken with a tiny sip of water, and some shouldn’t, so check what your team told you. If you’ve got diabetes, it’s extra important to ask for specific advice. And if your surgery gets pushed back, don’t raid the vending machine—ask the nurse first. Basically, follow the fasting instructions exactly, even if they seem annoying. They’re there to keep you safe.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: Prior to operative intervention, patients must adhere to institution-specific peri‑operative fasting protocols to minimize the risk of pulmonary aspiration and to ensure optimal anesthetic conditions. Current peri‑operative recommendations generally require cessation of solid food, including meat, fried foods, and milk‑based products, for a minimum of six hours before induction of anesthesia; fatty meals may necessitate an eight‑hour interval. Clear liquids (water, apple juice, black tea or coffee without milk, oral rehydration solutions, and carbohydrate drinks) are typically permitted until two hours before arrival for surgery. Patients should avoid chewing gum, candies, and tobacco products during the fasting interval, as these can increase gastric secretions and acidity. Essential medications should be taken with a small sip of water at the time specified by the anesthesiology team; diabetic patients must receive individualized instructions regarding insulin and oral hypoglycemic agents. Nonadherence may result in increased aspiration risk, intraoperative complications, or procedure postponement. If there is any uncertainty—recent oral intake, gastrointestinal symptoms, or deviation from the prescribed regimen—patients are advised to contact the preoperative clinic or anesthesiologist promptly for tailored guidance.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: Hey—quick run‑through of the fasting rules before your surgery so you don’t get stuck with a delay. No solid food for about six hours before you go under—think no sandwiches, milk, or anything heavy (greasy stuff can take even longer to clear). You can sip clear drinks up until roughly two hours beforehand: plain water, apple juice, black coffee or tea (but skip the milk or cream), and those clear sports drinks or carb drinks your clinic might give you. Don’t chew gum, suck on candy, smoke, or vape—those things screw with your stomach and can mess up anesthesia. If you take meds every morning, take them with a small sip of water unless the nurse told you otherwise. If you have diabetes, are pregnant, or aren’t sure when you last ate, call the preop nurse right away—there are special rules. If you accidentally eat or drink, tell staff; swallowing something could mean they postpone the surgery. Better to check than show up and get turned away.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: This preoperative communication delineates the fasting requirements for patients undergoing elective surgical procedures. Adherence to evidence-based preanesthetic fasting guidelines minimizes the risk of pulmonary aspiration and perioperative complications. Patients are instructed to observe NPO (nothing by mouth) status from midnight prior to the scheduled procedure unless otherwise directed by the anesthesia team. Solid foods should be avoided for a minimum of six to eight hours before induction of anesthesia. Non-clear liquids, such as milk-based beverages and beverages with pulp, should be withheld for at least six to eight hours; clear fluids (water, thin tea, black coffee without milk) may be consumed up to two hours before induction. Breast milk may be permitted for infants up to a specified time before anesthesia as per pediatric protocols. Special populations require individualized adjustments: individuals with diabetes may receive modified guidelines and receive glucose-containing fluids only as approved by the anesthesia team; patients with gastroesophageal reflux disease may require particular timing. Patients should avoid chewing gum or hard candies on the day of surgery as it can trigger salivation and complicate airway management. Prior to the procedure, clinicians should confirm understanding using teach-back, provide written instructions, and document the patient's assent. If acute symptoms such as vomiting or dehydration occur, contact the surgical team immediately. These instructions should be reviewed in the preoperative clinic and reinforced by nursing staff on the day of admission.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: When you’re prepping for surgery, one of the big things is fasting. In plain terms: think of your stomach as an empty suitcase. You don’t want food or big drinks in there when the anesthesia team starts the show. Generally, you shouldn’t eat solid foods for about six to eight hours before the operation. If you’ve got clear liquids, you can sip water, plain tea, or black coffee up to about two hours before anesthesia. Dairy-heavy drinks or juice with pulp should be avoided for at least six to eight hours before, and no chewing gum on the day of surgery, because it can mess with your airway. Your exact rules will come from your surgical team, and they may tweak things for kids, diabetics, or people with reflux. If you’re unsure, ask. You’ll usually get a simple checklist or a printed note you can take home. During your pre-op visit, the nurse will go over the times and sign-off that you understand. If you feel nauseous, dizzy, or sick, reach out to the staff right away. The point is to stay consistent with the plan so your anesthesia goes smoothly and the surgeon can get you started on time.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: Peri-operative fasting is a critical component of pre-surgical preparation, designed to mitigate the risk of aspiration and other peri-anesthetic complications. The standard protocol generally mandates that patients abstain from solid foods for a minimum of six hours and liquids for at least two hours prior to surgery. This precaution is crucial, as the presence of food or liquid in the stomach during anesthesia can lead to severe respiratory complications. Patients are typically advised to adhere strictly to these guidelines to ensure an optimal surgical outcome. Furthermore, specific instructions may vary based on the type of surgery, the patient's medical history, and any concurrent medical conditions. Healthcare providers must communicate these instructions clearly and confirm patient comprehension to prevent any inadvertent deviations from the prescribed fasting regimen. Examples of acceptable clear liquids include water, clear tea, and apple juice, whereas milk, coffee with milk, and solid foods must be strictly avoided. The adherence to these instructions is paramount for patient safety and the overall success of the surgical procedure.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: Hey there! So, if you're getting ready for surgery, you gotta know about this fasting thing. Basically, you shouldn't eat any solid food for about six hours before the surgery. And when it comes to drinks, stick to clear stuff like water or apple juice, but only up to two hours before. No milk or anything like that! This whole fasting deal is super important because eating or drinking too close to your surgery can cause some serious problems when you're under anesthesia. You definitely don't want any food or liquid in your stomach during that time, trust me. Your doctor will give you the lowdown on exactly what you can and can't have, so make sure you get it straight from them and follow it to the letter. It's all about keeping you safe and making sure everything goes smoothly in the operating room. So, just hang in there and follow the rules—they're there to help you out!
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: To ensure optimal patient safety and mitigate the risk of pulmonary aspiration during the administration of general anesthesia, strict adherence to peri-operative fasting guidelines is imperative. Current anesthesiology protocols dictate specific fasting intervals based on the type of ingested material. Patients are required to abstain from heavy, fatty, or fried meals for a minimum of eight hours prior to the scheduled induction of anesthesia. A light meal, consisting of non-fatty solids such as dry toast or crackers, may be consumed up to six hours pre-operatively. Furthermore, the consumption of clear liquids—defined as water, black coffee, or pulp-free apple juice—is permissible up to two hours before the surgical intervention. Patients must be explicitly counseled that failure to comply with these preoperative instructions significantly elevates the morbidity associated with regurgitation and aspiration pneumonitis, and will consequently result in the postponement or cancellation of the surgical procedure. Clear, documented communication of these instructions is a critical component of peri-operative nursing and anesthetic care.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: Hey there! So, before we get you ready for your surgery tomorrow, we need to go over the eating and drinking rules. It's super important you follow these so you don't get sick to your stomach while you're asleep, which can be really dangerous. Basically, you can't have a big, heavy meal—like a burger or anything fried—for at least eight hours before your surgery time. If you want a small snack, like a piece of plain toast, you can have that up to six hours beforehand. Now, for drinks, you can actually have clear liquids up until two hours before we take you back. When I say clear liquids, I mean stuff you can see through, like water, black coffee (no cream or sugar!), or apple juice. But please, no milk or orange juice with pulp. I know fasting is a total drag and you'll probably be pretty hungry, but it's totally necessary to keep you safe. If you end up sneaking a snack, we'll have to cancel the surgery, and nobody wants that! Let me know if you have any questions.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: Adherence to preoperative fasting guidelines is a critical component of perioperative safety, specifically regarding the mitigation of pulmonary aspiration risks during the induction of general anesthesia. Patients are strictly instructed to observe a 'nil per os' (NPO) status starting at midnight prior to the scheduled surgical intervention. This protocol necessitates the cessation of all solid food intake, including small snacks or gum, for a minimum of eight hours pre-procedure. Regarding clear liquids—defined as water, black coffee without milk, or pulp-free fruit juices—consumption is permissible up to two hours before the induction of anesthesia. The physiological rationale for these mandates involves ensuring the stomach is sufficiently empty to prevent the regurgitation of gastric contents into the respiratory tract while the patient’s protective airway reflexes are pharmacologically suppressed. Failure to comply with these clinical standards may result in the immediate postponement or cancellation of the surgical procedure to ensure patient hemodynamic stability and respiratory integrity. Patients should also consult their attending anesthesiologist regarding the administration of chronic medications with a minimal volume of water on the morning of surgery.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: Alright, so here is the deal for your surgery tomorrow: you’ve gotta make sure you don't eat anything at all once you hit the hay tonight. We call it 'fasting,' but basically, it just means no midnight snacks, no breakfast, and definitely no sneaking a piece of toast in the morning. If you’ve got stuff in your stomach when we put you under, it can actually be pretty dangerous because you might end up breathing that food into your lungs while you're knocked out. That is definitely not something you want to deal with. You can have a little bit of water or maybe some plain black coffee—no cream or sugar, though!—up until about two hours before you show up at the hospital. But seriously, once that clock hits the cutoff, stay away from the fridge. If you break the rules and grab a bite, even just a tiny bit of gum or a cracker, we’re gonna have to call the whole thing off and reschedule for another day. We just want to keep you safe while you're in the OR, so hang in there and wait until after the procedure to grab a meal!
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text:
To ensure patient safety during your upcoming procedure, strict adherence to peri-operative fasting guidelines is mandatory. When a patient is under general anaesthesia, the body's natural protective reflexes, such as coughing and swallowing, are suppressed. This creates a significant risk of pulmonary aspiration, a serious complication where gastric contents are regurgitated from the stomach and enter the lungs. Aspiration can lead to severe chemical pneumonitis or bacterial pneumonia, potentially requiring intensive care and prolonging your hospital stay. 

Therefore, you must be “Nil Per Os” (NPO), meaning nothing by mouth, for a specified period. The standard protocol requires abstaining from all solid foods and opaque liquids (e.g., milk, creamer, orange juice with pulp) for at least eight hours prior to your scheduled surgery. In some cases, clear fluids like water, black coffee, or apple juice may be consumed up to two hours before your arrival. Failure to comply with these instructions will likely result in the postponement of your surgery, as your safety is our paramount concern.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text:
So, why do we ask you not to eat or drink before your surgery? It's all about keeping you safe while you're asleep! When you go under for your operation, your body completely relaxes. That includes the little muscular flap in your throat that normally stops food or drink from going down the wrong way into your lungs. If your stomach has anything in it, that stuff could travel back up your throat and accidentally spill into your lungs. This is really dangerous and can cause a nasty lung infection, making your recovery much harder.

To avoid this, we need your tummy to be totally empty. The general rule is simple: no food, no milk, no juice with bits, and no coffee with creamer after midnight the night before your surgery. We might tell you it's okay to have a few sips of clear liquids like water or black tea a couple of hours beforehand, but please check with us. And don't try to sneak a mint or a piece of gum! We're not just being mean, we promise. If you eat, we’ll have to delay your surgery, and nobody wants that.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: Peri-operative fasting protocols are established to minimize the risk of pulmonary aspiration during anesthesia induction and maintenance. Prior to elective surgical procedures, patients must adhere to specific nil per os (NPO) guidelines to ensure adequate gastric emptying and reduce peri-operative morbidity. Current evidence-based standards, as endorsed by the American Society of Anesthesiologists, recommend complete abstention from solid food for a minimum of six to eight hours preceding the scheduled surgical intervention. Additionally, clear liquids—including water, plain tea, or coffee without milk—may be consumed up to two hours prior to anesthesia administration, provided they contain no particulate matter, nutritional supplements, or caloric additives. Patients must strictly avoid alcohol, tobacco products, and chewing gum during the designated fasting period. These precautions are critical because general anesthesia and sedative agents suppress protective airway reflexes; subsequent regurgitation of acidic gastric contents can result in chemical pneumonitis, acute respiratory distress, and substantial mortality. Healthcare providers must thoroughly document fasting compliance and systematically educate patients regarding the physiological rationale underlying these restrictions. Furthermore, individual physiological variations, such as delayed gastric emptying secondary to diabetes mellitus, gastroparesis, or chronic opioid use, may necessitate modified protocols or extended fasting intervals. Strict adherence to these standardized guidelines substantially reduces aspiration-related complications and optimizes surgical outcomes.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: Hey there! So here's the deal with the whole fasting thing before your surgery. I know it sounds like a drag, but you've gotta keep your stomach totally empty before they put you under. Think of it this way: when the anesthesia kicks in and knocks you out, your body's swallowing reflex basically takes a nap too. If there's food sitting in your belly, it could come back up and get into your lungs, which is super dangerous. So here's the easy version: no solid food for at least eight hours before you go in. That means no breakfast if you're having afternoon surgery! But here's some good news—you can have clear liquids like water or plain black coffee up until two hours before your appointment. Just nothing milky, no orange juice with pulp, and definitely no sneaking in snacks! No gum either, sorry. If you're diabetic or have tummy troubles, we might need to tweak these rules a bit. Just stick to the plan and you'll be fine. Your stomach might grumble, but safety comes first!
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: Peri-operative fasting instructions are a critical aspect of surgical preparation, aimed at minimizing the risk of aspiration pneumonia and other complications during anesthesia. Patients are typically instructed to abstain from solid foods for a minimum of six hours prior to the scheduled procedure. Clear liquids, such as water, black coffee, or apple juice, may be permitted up to two hours before surgery. It is essential to adhere strictly to these guidelines to ensure patient safety and optimize surgical outcomes. Any deviations should be communicated promptly to the healthcare team. Medications, including prescription and over-the-counter drugs, should be discussed with the anesthesiologist or surgeon, as some may need to be taken with a sip of water on the day of surgery.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: Hey there! Before you go under the knife, there are some important rules about eating and drinking you gotta follow. It's all about keeping you safe during the procedure. So, no solids for at least 6 hours before your surgery, okay? That means no midnight snacks! But, you can have clear liquids like water or apple juice up to 2 hours before. Just remember, no milk or orange juice, they don't count as clear. Stick to these rules so everything goes smoothly. If you're taking any meds, make sure to chat with your doctor about 'em. Some might be okay to take with a little water on the day of your surgery.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: Preoperative fasting, commonly referred to as nil per os (NPO), constitutes a fundamental component of peri-operative care aimed at reducing the risk of pulmonary aspiration during anesthesia. Evidence-based guidelines from the American Society of Anesthesiologists (ASA) recommend that adult patients may ingest clear liquids—defined as water, pulp-free apple juice, black coffee, and tea—up to two hours before induction. In contrast, intake of a light meal (for example, toast with jam and 120 ml of juice) should cease at least six hours prior to anesthesia, while consumption of high-fat or fried foods requires an eight-hour fasting interval due to prolonged gastric emptying. Pediatric protocols mirror these intervals, adjusted for age and metabolic considerations. Empirical data demonstrate that administration of 200 ml of water two hours preoperatively reduces gastric volume without significantly altering pH, thereby diminishing aspiration risk. It is incumbent upon multidisciplinary care teams to deliver standardized verbal and written instructions, supplemented with visual aids and checklists, to optimize patient comprehension and compliance. Documentation of fasting status in the preoperative note and verification immediately before transfer to the operating room ensure adherence and minimize peri-operative complications.
topic: Explaining peri-operative fasting instructions to surgical patients

text: Hey everyone! If you’ve got surgery coming up, here’s the lowdown on fasting so things go smoothly in the OR. Basically, you can sip clear stuff—like water, apple juice without pulp, black coffee, or plain tea—up to two hours before you walk into the hospital. After that, everything’s off-limits so your stomach is empty when they knock you out. Six hours before, no light meals—think toast, cereal, or yogurt gone. And if you’re into greasy burgers or a heavy breakfast, stop eating eight hours before anesthesia; your tummy takes longer to settle after fatty grub. Kids follow the same rules but might get special tweaks depending on age and the anesthesiologist’s call. It’s a good idea to write these times down or set alarms—better safe than sorry! Skipping this could lead to delays or bumping your procedure altogether. Got questions? Reach out to the nurse or your doc before D-day, and they’ll clear things up. Stay on track, and you’ll be in and out of pre-op without a hitch!

text: Dear Residents, We are writing to formally inform you of the City of Maplewood's newly enacted Affordable Housing Initiative, which took effect on January 1, 2025. This comprehensive policy establishes several significant protections and benefits for qualifying tenants within city limits. Effective immediately, annual rent increases for units designated as affordable housing will be capped at 3% or the Consumer Price Index, whichever is lower. Additionally, tenants whose household income falls at or below 80% of the Area Median Income (AMI) may apply for the newly established Rental Assistance Fund, which provides up to $500 per month in supplemental housing support. The policy further mandates that landlords provide a minimum of 90 days' written notice prior to any lease non-renewal. Tenants are encouraged to review the full policy documentation available at the Maplewood Housing Authority's official website or by visiting the Municipal Services Building at 400 Civic Center Drive during regular business hours. Should you require further clarification or wish to determine your eligibility for available programs, please contact the Housing Authority directly at housing@maplewoodgov.org.
topic: Summarizing a city's new affordable housing policy to tenants

text: Hey neighbors! So the city just rolled out this new affordable housing deal and honestly? It's pretty good news for a lot of us. If you're not making a ton of money (they check based on something called AMI), you might even qualify for up to $500 a month to help cover rent. Like, that's real money! Oh, and here's another big one — if your landlord decides not to renew your lease, they now have to give you a heads-up at least 90 days in advance. No more surprise 'you gotta go' situations. To figure out if you qualify for any of this stuff, just hop onto the city's housing website or swing by their office downtown. You can also shoot them an email if you've got questions. Definitely worth checking out — don't leave money on the table!

text: Dear Residents, We are writing to inform you of the City of Maplewood's newly enacted Affordable Housing Initiative, which took effect on January 1, 2024. This comprehensive policy framework introduces several significant provisions designed to protect tenants and expand access to affordable housing throughout the municipality. Effective immediately, annual rent increases for qualifying units will be capped at 3% or the Consumer Price Index, whichever is lower. Additionally, tenants residing in units priced below 80% of the Area Median Income threshold will be eligible for supplemental housing assistance vouchers, administered through the Department of Housing and Community Development. The policy further mandates that landlords provide a minimum of 90 days written notice prior to any lease termination, with the exception of cases involving documented lease violations. Tenants who believe their rights under this ordinance have been violated are encouraged to submit a formal complaint to the Office of Tenant Affairs at 415-555-0192 or via the city's official online portal. We strongly recommend that all residents review the full policy documentation, available at City Hall and on the municipal website, to ensure comprehensive understanding of your rights and responsibilities under this new framework.

text:
Official Memorandum: Implementation of the Revised Affordable Housing Policy

Dear Residents,

The City Council has recently enacted Ordinance 2023-47, which establishes significant modifications to the municipal affordable housing framework. This correspondence serves to elucidate the principal components of this policy revision that may affect your tenancy.

Effective January 1, 2024, property owners must provide a minimum 90-day written notification prior to implementing any rent increases exceeding 5%. Additionally, the ordinance establishes a rent stabilization mechanism for qualifying units, limiting annual increases to the Consumer Price Index plus 3%, not to exceed 8% in any calendar year.

The legislation further mandates that 15% of units in new residential developments containing more than 50 units must be designated as affordable housing, with rental rates not exceeding 30% of the area median income for qualifying households.

A Tenant Protection Fund has been established to provide legal assistance for income-eligible residents facing eviction proceedings. Concurrently, a Housing Preservation Program will allocate $25 million annually toward rehabilitation of existing affordable housing stock.

For additional information regarding eligibility criteria or to determine if your residence is subject to these provisions, please contact the Department of Housing and Community Development at (555) 123-4567 or visit the official municipal website.

Respectfully,

Office of Housing Policy
topic: Summarizing a city's new affordable housing policy to tenants

text:
Hey there, neighbors!

So, the big news is that City Hall just rolled out some major changes to our housing rules, and I wanted to give you the scoop on what this means for you!

Starting January 1st, your landlord can't just spring a big rent hike on you out of nowhere. If they want to jack up your rent by more than 5%, they've gotta give you a heads-up at least 90 days before. Pretty sweet, right? Plus, there's now a cap on how much they can raise your rent each year – it's tied to inflation plus a bit extra, but can't go higher than 8%.

The city's also putting the squeeze on developers – any new big apartment buildings have to set aside 15% of their units for folks who don't make big bucks. And get this – they've set up a fund to help renters fight unfair evictions! There's also a chunk of money (we're talking $25 million!) going toward fixing up affordable housing that's already out there.

Wondering if these new rules apply to your place? Just give the housing folks a ring at 555-123-4567 or check out the city's website. They can walk you through all the details and see if you qualify for any of these new protections.

Hang in there!

Your Housing Team

text:
The Municipal Housing Authority is pleased to announce the implementation of the Equitable Housing Initiative, a comprehensive policy framework designed to address housing affordability challenges within our urban community. This policy establishes several key provisions intended to benefit current and prospective tenants.

First, rent stabilization measures have been implemented to limit annual increases to 3% for existing tenants, contingent upon unit maintenance standards compliance. Second, the policy mandates that developers allocate 15% of new residential units as affordable housing, defined as units priced at or below 80% of the area median income. Third, a tenant protection ordinance has been enacted to strengthen eviction protections and extend notice periods to 90 days for non-renewal.

Additionally, the city has established a $50 million Housing Trust Fund to support down payment assistance programs and rehabilitation of existing affordable stock. Eligible households earning between 50% and 120% of area median income may access these resources through a competitive application process administered by the Department of Housing and Community Development.

We encourage all tenants to review the detailed policy documentation available on our official website and attend information sessions scheduled throughout the month. Representatives will address questions regarding eligibility requirements, application procedures, and implementation timelines. These measures represent a significant commitment to ensuring housing accessibility across all income levels.

text:

The city also made it way harder for landlords to kick people out. You've got 90 days' notice now instead of getting booted out on short notice. It's about time!
